subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Islamic Golden Age Astronomy
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Islamic Golden Age Astronomy.

hook.text: While Europe stagnated, Islamic scholars preserved and advanced Greek astronomy for 500 years.

buildup.text: Al-Khwarizmi, al-Battani, and others refined star catalogs, corrected Ptolemy's errors, and invented new instruments.

discovery.text: The astrolabe—perfected in the Islamic world—could calculate star positions, tell time, and find the direction of Mecca.

twist.text: Many star names we use today—Aldebaran, Betelgeuse, Rigel—come directly from Arabic. The language of the sky is Arabic.

climax.text: When their texts reached Europe in the 12th century, they reignited Western astronomy and helped spark the Renaissance.

punchline.text: Five centuries of brilliance. Star names still carry their legacy.

quiz.question: How did Islamic Golden Age astronomers impact Western astronomy?
options: They preserved Greek knowledge, corrected errors, and transmitted it to Renaissance Europe
They built the first telescopes and discovered Jupiter's moons
They proved the Earth orbits the Sun before Copernicus
